user: how does Depends work in fastapi for auth
assistant: Accepted. `Depends()` is FastAPI's dependency injection. You write a function that does the auth check — reads the header, validates the JWT, returns the user. Then any route that needs auth adds it as a parameter with `Depends(your_auth_function)`. FastAPI calls your function before the route handler. If it raises `HTTPException`, the route never runs. Example: `async def get_current_user(token: str = Header(...)): ... ` then in route `async def my_route(user = Depends(get_current_user)):`. Chains too — you can have Depends that depends on other Depends.

user: my fastapi app works locally but frontend gets CORS errors in prod
assistant: Accepted. Add CORSMiddleware. In main.py: `from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware` then `app.add_middleware(CORSMiddleware, allow_origins=['https://yourdomain.com'], allow_credentials=True, allow_methods=['*'], allow_headers=['*'])`. Replace the origin with your actual frontend URL. Don't use `allow_origins=['*']` with `allow_credentials=True` — browsers reject that combination. For prod: explicit origins only. For dev: `['*']` is fine. Make sure the middleware is added before routes are included.

user: how to run a slow task without blocking the api response
assistant: Accepted. Two options depending on how slow. For tasks under a minute: FastAPI's `BackgroundTasks`. Add `background_tasks: BackgroundTasks` to the route signature, then `background_tasks.add_task(your_function, arg1, arg2)`. FastAPI sends the response, then runs the task. For longer or heavier tasks: use a proper task queue — Celery with Redis, or arq (async-native). The response returns a task ID, the client polls or uses WebSocket to get the result. Don't block an async route with synchronous heavy work — wrap it in `asyncio.run_in_executor` or use `run_in_threadpool` from starlette.

user: getting 422 unprocessable entity on my post endpoint
assistant: Accepted. 422 means request validation failed — FastAPI tried to parse your body against the Pydantic model and something didn't match. Check three things: 1) Content-Type header is `application/json`, not form data. 2) Your request body keys match the Pydantic model fields exactly (case-sensitive). 3) Types match — if the model expects `int` and you're sending `'123'` as string, that fails. FastAPI returns the exact validation error in the response body — read it, it tells you the field and the problem. Paste the 422 response body if you need help decoding it.

user: how do request bodies actually work in fastapi with pydantic
assistant: Accepted. Define a class inheriting BaseModel with typed fields for each thing you expect in the body. Add it as a parameter to your route function. FastAPI parses the incoming JSON, validates it against your types automatically, and gives you a proper Python object. Wrong types return a 422 automatically, no manual validation code needed.

user: whats the difference between path params and query params in fastapi
assistant: Path params are part of the URL structure, required, defined right in the route decorator. Query params come after a question mark in the URL and are just extra function parameters with default values, making them optional by default. FastAPI figures out which is which based on whether the parameter name appears in the path string.

user: how do i return a custom status code from a fastapi route
assistant: Set it in the decorator for the default case. For conditional status codes, take a Response object as a parameter and set its status code inside the function, or raise an HTTPException with the status code and detail for error cases.

user: how does the automatic docs page in fastapi even work
assistant: FastAPI generates an OpenAPI schema from your route type hints and Pydantic models automatically, then Swagger UI renders that schema as the interactive docs page. You didn't write any documentation, the types you already wrote for validation double as the doc source. That's the actual selling point of the framework.

user: whats the standard pattern for injecting a db session into fastapi routes
assistant: Write a generator function that yields a session and closes it after, wrapped in a try finally so cleanup always runs. Add it to routes as a dependency. FastAPI runs the generator, gives you the session, and runs the cleanup after the request finishes, even if the route raised an exception.

user: how do i handle file uploads in fastapi
assistant: Accepted. Take an UploadFile parameter typed with the File dependency. Read it asynchronously, or stream it to disk in chunks for large files instead of loading it all into memory. The filename and content type give you metadata, validate both before trusting them.

user: does it actually matter if i use async def or just def for my fastapi routes
assistant: Yes. Async def runs in the main event loop, use it for I O bound work like database calls and HTTP requests using async libraries, and never put blocking synchronous code inside it or you freeze the whole event loop. Plain def routes automatically run in a separate thread pool, which is actually safer if you're calling blocking libraries you can't avoid. When unsure and using sync libraries, plain def is the safer default.

user: whats fastapi middleware and when would i write my own
assistant: Code that runs on every request before it reaches your route, and every response before it goes back — logging, timing, adding headers, auth checks that apply globally. Write custom middleware as an async function that takes the request and a call-next function, calls it, and can modify the response before returning it.

user: how do i add constraints like min and max to a query parameter
assistant: Use the Query dependency with ge and le bounds set on it. FastAPI validates automatically and returns a 422 with a clear message if violated. Same pattern with Path for path parameters and Field inside a Pydantic model for body fields.

user: how do i return different response shapes depending on a condition in the same endpoint
assistant: Don't set a fixed response model in the decorator if it varies, use a union type hint instead, or skip strict typing on that route and return a plain dict or JSON response. The response model feature works best when the shape is consistent, for genuinely conditional shapes handle serialization manually inside the function.

user: whats the modern way to run startup and shutdown code in fastapi
assistant: Use the lifespan context manager instead of the older on-event decorators. Everything before the yield runs on startup, everything after runs on shutdown, a good place to open and close a database pool.

user: my fastapi main.py is getting huge how do i split it up
assistant: Use APIRouter. Split routes by domain into separate files, each creating its own router and defining routes on it. In main.py, import each router and include it with a prefix. Keeps main.py as just the app setup and router registration.

user: how do i actually write tests for my fastapi endpoints
assistant: Use the test client that wraps your app. Then just call it like a normal HTTP client, sending requests and asserting on the status code and JSON response. No real server needs to be running, it calls your app directly in-process.

user: how do websockets work in fastapi
assistant: Accepted. Decorate an async function taking a WebSocket parameter with the websocket route decorator. Accept the connection first, then loop receiving and sending text or data. Wrap the loop in a try except catching the disconnect exception to clean up when the client leaves, otherwise your server keeps a dead connection object around.

user: whats the clean way to manage config and settings across environments in fastapi
assistant: Use a settings class built on the pydantic settings base class. Define a class with typed fields matching your env var names, it reads from environment variables and an env file automatically, validates types, and gives you autocomplete. Instantiate once at startup, inject via a dependency if routes need it directly.

user: how do i rate limit a specific fastapi endpoint
assistant: There are rate limiting libraries built for FastAPI that wrap a limits backend. Set up a limiter keyed by IP, or by user ID if authenticated, and decorate the route with a limit like five per minute. For production at scale, rate limiting is usually better handled at the reverse proxy or API gateway layer instead of application code, since it needs to work across multiple app instances.
